coredata.dc:title: Metal-organic frameworks-derived zinc oxide nanopolyhedra/S, N: graphene quantum dots/polyaniline ternary nanohybrid for high-performance acetone sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dongzhi
Wu, Zhenling
Zong, Xiaoqi
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks-derived zinc oxide nanopolyhedra/S, N: graphene quantum dots/polyaniline (ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI) ternary nanohybrid was synthesized by in-situ polymerization route. The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite was fabricated on a flexible polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate with interdigital electrodes. The acetone-sensing properties of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite sensor were investigated by exposing it to various concentrations of acetone at room temperature. The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor demonstrated high response, fast response/recovery time, stable repeatability, excellent selectivity, outstanding long-term stability, and ppb-level sensitivity for trace acetone detection at room temperature. These results render the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor a promising candidate for acetone sensing at room temperature. The underlying sensing mechanism of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor toward acetone gas ascribed to the synergistic effect and heterojunction created in the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S, N: graphene quantum dots
Polyaniline
Metal-organic framework
In-situ polymerization
Acetone sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanohybrid
Synthesis of S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N: GQDs)
Synthesis of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of (a) S, N: GQDs, (b) ZnO and (c) drop-casting fabrication of sensor prototype.
(a) X-ray diffractograms of ZnO, PANI, S, N: GQDs and ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI samples. (b) EDS spectrum of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI samples.
XPS spectra of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sample: (a) survey spectrum, (b) C 1s spectrum, (c) O 1s spectrum, (d) N 1s spectrum, (e) Zn 2p spectrum, (f) S 2p spectrum.
FT-IR spectra of ZnO, PANI, S, N: GQDs and ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI samples.
SEM images of (a) PANI, (b) PANI/S, N: GQDs, (c) ZnO and (d) ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI samples.
TEM images of (a) PANI, (b) ZnO, (c) ZnO/S, N: GQDs, (d) ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI, HRTEM images of (e–f) ZnO, ZnO/S, N: GQDs samples.

(a) Response of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor toward acetone at room temperature of 25 °C. (b) Dynamic response-recovery curves. (c) The sensor response as a function of gas concentration. (d) Dynamic resistance transition toward 50 ppm of acetone gas.

(a) Real-time resistance measurement of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor toward acetone gas, and (b) response of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor toward step-increased acetone concentration and recovery in air. (c) Repeatability of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor toward 2, 10 and 20 ppm acetone gas, and (d) the selectivity of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor.

(a) Long term stability for 2, 5, and 20 ppm acetone of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor. (b) Response of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor as a function of relative humidity. (c) Gas sensing response of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor toward 1 ppm acetone after some bending cycles.
(a–b) Mechanism diagram of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite toward acetone sensing. (c) p-n heterojunction in the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite.
Performance comparison of the presented sensor in this work with previous reports.

head.ce:title: Metal-organic frameworks-derived zinc oxide nanopolyhedra/S, N: graphene quantum dots/polyaniline ternary nanohybrid for high-performance acetone sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Unconventional Oil & Gas Development, Ministry of Education, College of Information and Control Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Unconventional Oil & Gas Development
College of Information and Control Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic frameworks-derived zinc oxide nanopolyhedra/S, N: graphene quantum dots/polyaniline (ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI) ternary nanohybrid was synthesized by in-situ polymerization route. The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite was fabricated on a flexible polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate with interdigital electrodes. The acetone-sensing properties of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite sensor were investigated by exposing it to various concentrations of acetone at room temperature. The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor demonstrated high response, fast response/recovery time, stable repeatability, excellent selectivity, outstanding long-term stability, and ppb-level sensitivity for trace acetone detection at room temperature. These results render the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor a promising candidate for acetone sensing at room temperature. The underlying sensing mechanism of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor toward acetone gas ascribed to the synergistic effect and heterojunction created in the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:para: The early diagnosis of human disease can be realized by detecting biomarker gases in exhaled human breath [1]. Acetone gas, serving as a human breath marker for some diseases such as diabetes and halitosis, may offer a valuable reference for the early diagnosis of these diseases [2,3]. For example, more than 1.8 ppm of acetone gas can be found in the diabetics, compared with 0.2-0.5 ppm of acetone gas in exhaled breath of healthy people. Moreover, the component of formaldehyde, hydrogen sulfide and trimethylamine in exhaled breath have strong correlations with the lung cancer, halitosis and trimethylaminuria, respectively [4–6]. Furthermore, the detection of acetone gas is also of great significance in various applications, such as industrial production, environmental monitoring and volatile poisonous chemical gas leakage. Hence, a reliable sensor with large detection range and low limit of detection is greatly required in various fields.

Recently, metal oxide has received great attention due to their unique properties, such as its incredibly high surface area, ultrahigh porosity, and tunable structures [7]. M.M. Arafat et al. developed a robust and novel approach for 1-D core-shell TiO2-Al2O3 nanostructures and used for gas sensing applications [8]. Meng et al. synthesized Au/SnO2/RGO nanocomposites by a one-step method and the Au/SnO2/RGO nanocomposites exhibited higher response, wide detection range and high reproducibility to ethanol [9]. Many works have been taken to develop the MOFs-derived metal oxides, which possess high surface area and high gas accessible structures due to their inherent open porosity [10]. For example, Lü et al. developed Co doped MOFs-templated porous Co3O4 concave nanotubes for ethanol sensing at high temperature [11]. Jeong et al. synthesized Co3O4-SnO2 hollow nanostructures for methylbenzene gas sensors [12]. However, MOFs-drived metal oxides need to work at high temperature, typically in the range of 200–500 °C, which can cause high power consumption [13].

Graphene, a novel 2D nanocarbon material, has recently been widely studied for gas sensing due to its large specific surface area, strong gas adsorption capacity, high carrier mobility and excellent electronic conductivity [14–16]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are much smaller than graphene sheets, also have been attracted a lot of attention due to their unique quantum confinement and edge effects [17]. For instance, Hosseini et al. developed a highly sensitive flexible humidity sensors based on GQDs, which showed good selectivity and response [18]. Ruiz et al. fabricated a room temperature (RT) GQD-based resistive sensor by drop-casting technology [19].

Currently, conducting polymers have been used for gas detection due to their easy synthesis, environmental stability and reversible redox reaction [20,21]. Conducting polymers such as PANI also have chemoresistive properties that can change upon exposure to acetone [22]. Hicks et al. reported an acetone gas sensor based on WO3/PANI composite, which was able to detect 10 ppm acetone at room temperature [23]. Ariyageadsakul et al. revealed a conductivity enhancement of PANI emeraldine salt for acetone gas detection [24].

In this paper, we synthesized MOFs-derived ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanohybrid by a facile one-step in-situ polymerization technique, which can provide strong catalytic activity and interaction for the produced composite via covalent bonding during the oxidative polymerization process [25]. The as-prepared samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), FT-IR (NEXUS FT-IR) spectrums, scanning electron microscope (SEM), energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). A high-performance acetone gas sensor was fabricated on a flexible PET substrate with interdigital electrodes. The measurement result demonstrated that the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor has high response value, fast response/recovery time, stable repeatability, excellent selectivity, and outstanding long-term stability. Finally, the underlying sensing mechanism of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor toward acetone was explored.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate [Zn(NO3)2·6(H2O)], methanol [CH3OH], thiourea [NH2CSNH2, ≥99%], citric acid [C6H8O7], sodium hydroxide [NaOH], ammonium peroxydisulfate (APS) [(NH4)2S2O8], aniline monomer [C6H7N] and hydrochloric acid [HCl] were obtained from Chinese Medicine Group Chemical Reagent, and 2-Methylimidazole [C4H6N2] was from Shanghai Titan Scientific.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanohybrid
Synthesis of S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N: GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The S, N: GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal process of citric acid and thiourea [26]. In a typical procedure of S, N: GQDs preparation, 0.21 g citric acid and 0.23 g thiourea were dissolved in 5 mL water, and stirred for 30 min. After that, the solution was transferred into a 20 mL autoclave and was heated at 160 °C for 4 h. Finally, the products were collected after centrifugal treatment at 5000 rpm for 30 min. Fig. 1 (a) shows the carbonization process of citric acid. First, the citric acid was gradually formed monodisperse GQDs precursors through a series of chemical reactions such as oxidative polymerization [26]. After heated for 4 h at 160 °C, S, N: GQDs was formed by incompletely carbonized citric acid and the addition of thiourea.

The ZIF-8 served as a template for the synthesis of ZnO nanopolyhedra via room temperature precipitation of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 2-methylimidazole (2-MeIM). As shown in Fig. 1(b), 0.582 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.6568 g of 2-MeIM were dissolved in 25 mL of methanol, respectively. Then, the two solutions were mixed together and being stirred for 30 min. After that, the resultant mixed solution was kept for 24 h at room temperature to complete the crystallization process. Finally, the precipitate (ZIF-8 nanopolyhedra) was obtained after centrifugation with 5000 rpm for 1 min and rinse with methanol. ZnO nanopolyhedra was synthesized by the calcination of ZIF-8 at 350 °C under nitrogen gas for 2 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite was synthesized via in-situ polymerization method [27]. Fig. 1(c) illustrates the preparation process of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite. In a typical process, 91.75 μL of aniline (1.02 g/mL), 2.5 mL of HCl (4.5 mol/L) and 1 mL of S, N: GQDs were mixed with 30 mL DI water. 0.225 g APS dissolved in 20 mL DI water was added dropwise into the above mixture. Polymerization process was performed in an ice-bath for 2 h. The colour of the solution changed from white to green, indicating the formation of PANI/S, N: GQDs. 1 g of ZnO powders and NaOH (0.1 M) were added in the solution and stirred in an ice-bath for 4 h to get the precipitant of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI. The greenish black ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI solution was obtained after washing several times. The sensor was fabricated by drop-casting ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film on a flexible polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate with Au/Cu interdigital electrodes (IDEs). In order to make comparison, the ZnO, ZnO/PANI and ZnO/S, N: GQDs sensors were prepared for reference by the similar route.

The sensing properties of the sensor were investigated by exposing it to different concentrations of acetone gas (0.5–50 ppm) at RT of 25 °C. The sensor resistance was recorded via a computer-controlled Agilent 34970 A data logger. The sensor response was determined by S = (Ra-Rg)/Ra × 100%, where Ra and Rg are the sensor resistance in air and acetone gas, respectively. The response/recovery time is defined as the time for the sensor to reach 90% of total resistance change from its initial resistance.

XRD measurement was performed on the as-prepared samples using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku D/Max2500PC, Japan) equipped with a Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The XRD characterization results are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The diffraction peaks of the samples were inspected in a range from 10° to 75°. The XRD pattern of PANI shows three prominent peak at 2θ = 14.8°, 20.2° and 25.4°, which are consistent with the (011), (020), and (200) planes [28–30]. The featured diffraction peaks indexed to PANI confirm the successful preparation of PANI. As shown in the XRD pattern of ZnO, all the diffraction peaks matched well with ZnO (JCPDS no. 36-1451). The diffraction peaks of ZnO are observed at 2θ of 31.8°, 34.5°, 36.1°, 47.5°, 56.5°, 62.6° and 67.8°, which correspond to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), and (112) planes, respectively [31,32]. The most intense peak of S, N: GQDs at 2θ = 23.1° correspond to the (002) reflections [26]. The XRD pattern of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite substantially contains the main characteristic peaks of the ZnO, S, N: GQDs and PANI, which confirm the presence of ZnO, S, N: GQDs and PANI in the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite. The EDS spectrum of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI is shown in Fig. 2(b), which illustrates the existence of C, O, N, Zn, and S without other impurity element.

XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS spectrometer) was employed to inspect the surface composition and chemical state of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite. The XPS survey spectrum is shown in Fig. 3 (a), which shows that the main constituent elements are C, N, O, Zn and S in the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sample. In Fig. 3(b), the C 1 s XPS spectrum shows a main peak at 284.95 eV due to the sp2-carbon, followed by two lower intensity peaks. The peak at 286.65 eV assigns to C–N, C–S and C–O, which is attributed to the sp3-hybridized carbon. The peak at 288.65 eV attributes to CO from carbonyls and carboxylates [26]. Fig. 3(c) presents the XPS spectrum of O 1 s. The peak at 531.91 eV attributes to Zn–O bonding. O 1 s peaks represent the oxygen atoms from S, N: GQDs and ZnO, respectively [28]. The N 1 s XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 3(d) has two distinct peaks at 398.7 eV and at 399.8 eV, which are ascribed to the amine group (–NH–) and cationic species (=NH+-), respectively. Two lower intensity peaks at 400.7 eV and 397.8 eV ascribes to the presence of NH2 +- and –N– groups, respectively. The XPS spectra of Zn is shown in Fig. 3(e), the Zn 2p spectrum shows two peaks binding energies at 1021.95 eV and 1045.01 eV, which corresponding to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(f), the peaks at 162.35 and 163.65 eV assign to the binding energies of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively [26].

The nanostructure and component of ZnO, PANI, S, N: GQDs, and ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI samples are further investigated by FT-IR spectrum (Thermo-Nicolet Nexus 670 FT-IR), and the FT-IR characterization results are shown in Fig. 4 . We can clearly find the PANI has several FT-IR characteristic absorption peaks. The peak at 3455 cm−1 is attributed to the presence of C–H stretching vibration, and the peaks at 1562 and 1478 cm−1 are corresponded to the CC and CN stretch of quinoid rings and benzenoid structure of PANI [28,33]. The peak at 1298 cm−1 assigned to C–N stretching mode of benzenoid unit, and the peaks at 1130 cm−1 and 820 cm−1 attributed to the C–H in-plane bending vibration and C–H out-of-plane bending vibration of the benzene ring [34,35]. The broad absorption band at 3455 cm−1 assigns to stretching vibrations of OH and NH, which indicates that there are lots of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of S, N: GQDs [36]. The bands at 1720 cm−1 and 1660 cm−1 assign to the vibrational absorption band of CO in COOH and CONH, respectively. The peaks at 1087 cm−1 and 635 cm−1 -attribute to the stretching CS and C–S, respectively [37]. For ZnO nanopolyhedra, the peak at 970 cm−1 attributes to the in-plane bending of the ring, and the peak at 750 cm−1 -attributes to out-of-plane bending [38]. The peak at 460 cm−1 ascribes to the vibration of Zn-O bond in ZnO [39]. The FT-IR characteristic absorption peaks of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite contain the above feature peaks of ZnO, PANI and S, N: GQDs, which manifest the successful fabrication of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the surface morphology of ZnO, S, N GQDs, PANI, and ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI was inspected by a field emission scanning electron microscope (Hitachi S-4800). Fig. 5 (a) shows nanorod-shaped PANI nanocapsules [28]. Fig. 5(b) shows the SEM image of PANI/S, N: GQDs, the S, N: GQDs decorated on PANI nanocapsules can be observed [40]. Fig. 5(c) shows that the ZnO nanopolyhedra has the typical rhombic dodecahedron morphology with a uniform particle size of 50 nm [41]. Fig. 5(d) shows the SEM image of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite. The ternary nanomaterials of PANI, ZnO and S, N: GQDs are wrapped together and formed a porous nanostructure.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100, Japan) was further used to examine the nanostructure of the as-prepared samples. Fig. 6 (a–d) shows the TEM images of PANI, ZnO, ZnO/S, N: GQDs, ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI samples, respectively. From Fig. 6(a) and (b), nanocapsule-shaped PANI and nanopolyhedra-shaped ZnO can be clearly observed. Fig. 6(c) and (d) shows the S, N: GQDs attached on the ZnO and PANI. The size of nanocapsule-shaped PANI is about 60 nm, and the size of ZnO is about 50 nm. Fig. 6(e–f) shows the HRTEM images of ZnO, ZnO/S, N: GQDs samples. The graphene quantum dots have point-like structures with size less than 10 nm. The lattice fringe of (002) plane for S, N: GQDs and (110) plane for ZnO are 0.35 nm and 0.28 nm, respectively [26,42].

Fig. 7 (a) shows the acetone-sensing response of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor compared with the ZnO, ZnO/S, N: GQDs and ZnO/PANI film sensors. The response values of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor are about 1.98%, 4.84%, 11.56%, 25.6%, 52.23%, 71.11% and 91.81% toward acetone gas of 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20 and 50 ppm, respectively. The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor has the highest response among the four sensors. Fig. 7(b) depicts the time-dependent response-recovery curves of these film sensors toward acetone gas. According to measurement result, the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor obtains much shorter response/recovery time than the other three sensors. The response/recovery times are about 15 s/27 s toward 2 ppm of acetone, and 22 s/30 s toward 5 ppm of acetone. Fig. 7(c) shows the responses of the four sensors as functions of acetone gas concentration. The fitting functions are given as Y = 93.57–95.71 × 0.92x, Y = 55.56–55.95 × 0.95x, Y = 47.51–47.87 × 0.95x and Y = 33.76–34.07 × 0.95x for the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI, ZnO/S, N: GQDs, ZnO/PANI and ZnO sensors, respectively. The correlation coefficients (R2) are 0.9958, 0.9895, 0.9980 and 0.9916, respectively. Fig. 7(d) exhibits the dynamic resistance of ZnO, ZnO/S, N: GQDs, ZnO/PANI and ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensors toward acetone gas (50 ppm) at RT. The base resistance of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs s/PANI sensor is decreased by the incorporation of ZnO, S, N: GQDs and PANI. The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor exhibited n-type sensing properties toward acetone gas.

Fig. 8 (a) demonstrates the real-time resistance measurement of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor toward acetone gas from 0.5 to 50 ppm at RT. The sensor resistance is monotonically decreased with the increasing of gas concentration. Fig. 8(b) presents the response of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor toward 1 ppm step-increased acetone concentration and recovery in air. Each exposure cycle was performed by an exposure interval of 50 s in acetone gas. It demonstrates that the sensor exhibits remarkable response to acetone gas as well as a good recovery characteristic in air. Fig. 8(c) shows the repeatability of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor measured toward acetone gas under the same conditions at room temperature. The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor was exposed to acetone gas at concentration of 2 ppm, 10 ppm, 20 ppm and air for five exposure/recovery cycles. The result shows that the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor possesses good repeatability. Fig. 8(d) illustrates the selectivity of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor toward 5 ppm of benzene, methane, ammonia and ethanol at room temperature, respectively. The measurement result suggests that the response of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor to acetone is obviously higher than other tested gases, which presents that ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor possesses high selectivity for acetone detection.

Fig. 9 (a) shows the long-term stability of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor toward 2, 5, and 20 ppm acetone. The response of the sensor was measured every 2 days over a month. The response of the sensor did not vary significantly with time passing, which confirms that the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor has a good long-term stability. We also investigated the humidity response of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor. As shown in Fig. 9(b), humidity has little effect on ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor. The sensor response is a linear function with the relative humidity, which can be given as Y=-15.07 + 0.83X. Therefore, humidity compensation can be made for the sensor toward the acetone detection under different humidity levels. Fig. 9(c) shows the response of the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor towards 1 ppm acetone gas after some bending cycles. We can find that there is no obvious change in the sensor response after bending test, demonstrating the reliable and robust flexibility of the device.

Table 1 lists the measurement results in this work compared with previous work. The detection limit, operating temperature, and response value are compared with the similar state-of-the-art materials based acetone gas sensor, which suggests the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor exhibits lower detection limit, higher response value at room temperature. The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI sensor is able to be a remarkable candidate for ppb-level acetone detection.

The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite sensor exhibited excellent acetone gas sensing properties. In the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI heterostructures, two different-type depletion layers would exist. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), the first depletion layer is formed by the adsorption of oxygen species located at the surface of ZnO, while another depletion layer associated with the ZnO and PANI heterojunction. The sensing properties of ZnO-based sensors are affected by the surface reaction between chemisorbed oxygen species [51]. The chemisorption of oxygen creates the electron depletion layer on the surface of ZnO [52]. When the ZnO exposed to air, oxygen molecules in air are adsorbed onto the surface of the sensing material and then produce active adsorbed oxygen species [53]. Consequently, a thicker electron depletion layer forms on the surface region, resulting in a high resistance of the sensor. As shown in Fig. 10(b), when the sensor exposed to acetone gas, the acetone molecules react with the former oxygen species to generate CO2 and H2O, and the trapped electrons are reinjected back to the conduction band of ZnO. The reaction is simplified as below [52]:
(1) CH3COCH3(gas) + O2 −(ads) → CH3C+O + CH3O− + e−
(2) CH3C+O → CH3 + + CO(gas)
(3) CO(gas) + O2 −(ads) → CO2 + e−

The enhancement in the acetone sensing properties of ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite ascribed greatly to the increase of active sites and the p-n heterojunction. As shown in SEM and TEM, the ZnO is modified by the S, N: GQDs and PANI nanocapsules. The porous nanostructure offers many reaction sites and allows gas molecules to diffuse both inside and outside of the film layers. More oxygen molecules adsorbed onto the surface of the ZnO and reacted with the acetone molecules, which results in a large resistance change in the film [54].

The formation of heterojunction at the interface between n-type ZnO and p-type PANI/S, N: GQDs plays an important role in improving the acetone sensing properties [55]. The band gap for PANI and ZnO are 4.49 eV and 3.37 eV, respectively. Self-established electronic field of depletion region created in the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI heterojunction due to electron-hole recombination. Charge conduction across the p-n interface will further contribute to the change of resistance [8,55]. As shown in Fig. 10(c), when the sensor exposed to acetone gas, the electrons of PANI/S, N: GQDs and the holes of the ZnO move in opposite directions until the new Fermi level reaches equilibrium. In this process, the electron transport between n-type ZnO and p-type PANI/S, N: GQDs overcomes the potential barrier due to the band bending, which results in a decrease of the depletion layer thickness and the resistance of the sensor [56]. The use of PANI in the composite induces the formation of p-n heterojunctions and leads to a redistribution of charge carriers at the interface of n-type ZnO and p-type PANI/S, N: GQDs, decreasing the activation energy and enthalpy required for the adsorption of acetone gas molecules [57]. The presence of S, N: GQDs in the film may create Schottky contact at the interface between GODs and ZnO, which can further enhance the sensor response through effectively capture and migration of electrons [58]. Shao et al. reported that the incorporation of GQDs based materials result in electronic sensitizations toward gas detection at room temperature [59]. Furthermore, the doping of PANI can provide much more gas adsorption sites for the ternary nanostructure [60,61]. Therefore, the enhanced acetone sensing properties ascribed to the synergistic effect and heterojunction created in the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite.

In summary, a high-performance actone gass sensor based on ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film was fabricated via in-situ polymerization technique. XRD, FT-IR, SEM, EDS, XPS and TEM measurements were employed to characterize the presented samples. The gas-sensing properties of the as-prepared sensors were investigated at room temperature upon exposure to different acetone concentrations. Experimental results showed that the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor exhibited a high response, good repeatability, excellent stability, outstanding selectivity and fast response/recovery characteristics towards low concentration acetone gas, which was superior to the pure ZnO, ZnO/S, N: GQDs and ZnO/PANI film sensor. The ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI film sensor was found to be a suitable candidate for detection of ppb-level acetone at room temperature, due to the synergistic effect and heterojunction created in the ZnO/S, N: GQDs/PANI nanocomposite.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51777215), the Key Research & Development Plan Project of Shandong Province (2018GSF117002), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (18CX07010A).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Breath analysis by nanostructured metal oxides as chemo-resistive gas sensors
Mater. Today
Highly sensitive acetone sensors based on Y-doped SnO2 prismatic hollow nanofibers synthesized by electrospinning
Sens. Actuators B: Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dongzhi Zhang received his B.S. degree from Shandong University of Technology in 2004, M.S. degree from China University of Petroleum in 2007, and obtained Ph.D. degree from South China University of Technology in 2011. He is currently an associate professor at China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, China. His fields of interests are gas and humidity sensing materials, nanotechnology, and polymer electronics.

Zhenling Wu received his B.S. degree from Qingdao University in 2016. Currently, he is a graduate student at China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, China. His fields of interests include nanomaterials based gas sensors, precision measurement technology.

Xiaoqi Zong received her B.S. degree from China University of Petroleum (East China) in 2016. Currently, she is a graduate student at China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, China. Her fields of interests include nanomaterials-based humidity and gas sensors, precision measurement technology.
